_meta.title: GTCI 2025 — Ministry & Source Intelligence Map
_meta.description: All 77 GTCI 2025 indicators mapped to responsible Brunei ministries/agencies.
_meta.note: Codes match original CSV format (X.Y.Z). Null values shown as 'NA'.
_meta.code_format: X.Y.Z — X=pillar group (1-6), Y=sub-pillar, Z=indicator number

data_types.SURVEY: Perception or opinion survey (e.g. WEF Executive Opinion Survey, IMD World Competitiveness Survey)
data_types.HARD_DATA: Official statistics, administrative data, or measured empirical figures
data_types.INDEX: Sub-index or composite score derived from another ranked index
data_types.COMPOSITE: GTCI's own computed composite from multiple sub-components

ministries.name: Ministry of Education
ministries.description: Responsible for formal education, higher education, lifelong learning, and research & innovation indicators. (11 primary-owned indicators; 7 additional indicators where MOE is a secondary/co-owning stakeholder for education-relevant workforce and innovation outcomes.)

ministries.name: Ministry of Finance and Economy
ministries.description: Responsible for economic competitiveness, business environment, financial infrastructure, labour market regulation, and economic openness indicators.

ministries.name: Ministry of Transport and Infocommunications
ministries.description: Responsible for ICT infrastructure, digital connectivity, broadband access, and e-government indicators.

ministries.name: Ministry of Home Affairs
ministries.description: Responsible for immigration, personal safety, rule of law, and civil liberties indicators.

ministries.name: Ministry of Health
ministries.description: Responsible for health outcomes, life expectancy, and workforce health indicators.

ministries.name: Ministry of Culture, Youth and Sports
ministries.description: Responsible for quality of life, social cohesion, tolerance, and cultural openness indicators.

ministries.name: Prime Minister's Office
ministries.description: Responsible for governance quality, institutional effectiveness, and national strategy indicators.

ministries.name: Ministry of Development
ministries.description: Responsible for infrastructure development, environmental sustainability, and urban planning indicators.

ministries.name: Multi-Ministry / Cross-Cutting
ministries.description: Indicators that span multiple ministries and require inter-agency coordination.

data_sources.name: WEF Executive Opinion Survey
data_sources.description: Annual survey of business executives assessing institutional quality, business environment, and governance. Conducted by the World Economic Forum.
data_sources.submission_mechanism: Survey responses submitted via WEF country partner (in Brunei: BCCI / AMBD). Brunei must increase business executive respondent base.
data_sources.brunei_contact: Brunei Darussalam National Business Council / AMBD

data_sources.name: IMD World Competitiveness Yearbook
data_sources.description: Annual competitiveness assessment combining hard data (2/3) with executive survey (1/3). Covers 64 economies.
data_sources.submission_mechanism: Hard data submitted by national authorities; survey via IMD partner institutions. Brunei participates through DEPS.

data_sources.name: World Bank — Worldwide Governance Indicators
data_sources.description: Six composite governance dimensions: Voice & Accountability, Political Stability, Government Effectiveness, Regulatory Quality, Rule of Law, Control of Corruption.
data_sources.submission_mechanism: Composite from 30+ underlying sources. Brunei can influence via improving source data and national statistics quality.

data_sources.name: UNESCO Institute for Statistics
data_sources.description: Official education statistics including gross enrolment ratios, literacy rates, government expenditure on education, and pupil-teacher ratios.
data_sources.submission_mechanism: Data submitted by Ministry of Education annually to UNESCO. Brunei must ensure timely and complete submission of education administrative data.

data_sources.name: ILO — ILOSTAT Database
data_sources.description: International Labour Organization statistics on labour force participation, unemployment, wages, and working conditions.
data_sources.submission_mechanism: Data submitted via Department of Labour / Department of Economic Planning & Statistics through ILO STAT database protocols.

data_sources.name: ITU — ICT Statistics
data_sources.description: International Telecommunication Union data on broadband access, mobile subscriptions, internet penetration, and ICT infrastructure.
data_sources.submission_mechanism: Data submitted by AITI / MTIC annually through ITU national data reporting questionnaire.
data_sources.brunei_contact: Authority for Info-communications Technology Industry (AITI) / MTIC

data_sources.name: World Bank — Human Capital Index
data_sources.description: Composite index measuring outcomes in health and education that a child born today can expect to attain by age 18.
data_sources.submission_mechanism: Composite derived from health and education statistical submissions. Brunei must improve data completeness to MOH/UNESCO pipelines.
data_sources.brunei_contact: Ministry of Health / Ministry of Education

data_sources.name: OECD — PISA / Education at a Glance
data_sources.description: Programme for International Student Assessment and Education at a Glance statistics. Brunei participates in PISA.
data_sources.submission_mechanism: PISA: students sit standardised tests coordinated by MoE. Education at a Glance: data submitted to OECD by MoE. Brunei participates but should increase PISA participant cohort.
data_sources.brunei_contact: Ministry of Education — Curriculum Development Department

data_sources.name: Transparency International — CPI
data_sources.description: Corruption Perceptions Index — composite of expert assessments and surveys on public sector corruption.
data_sources.submission_mechanism: Derived from 13 underlying sources. Brunei influenced by improving ACB visibility, public reporting, and participation in underlying source surveys.

data_sources.name: Freedom House — Freedom in the World / Press
data_sources.description: Annual assessments of political rights, civil liberties, and press freedom.
data_sources.submission_mechanism: Expert-assessed. Brunei's score reflects legal framework, civil liberties constraints. Limited direct submission pathway — improvement requires policy reform.

data_sources.name: Global Innovation Index
data_sources.description: WIPO composite index on innovation inputs and outputs including R&D, patents, and knowledge creation.
data_sources.submission_mechanism: Composite from national R&D statistics, patent data, and publication data. Brunei must submit R&D expenditure to WIPO and increase patent filings.
data_sources.brunei_contact: Ministry of Finance and Economy / DEPS / Research & Innovation Agencies

data_sources.name: IMD World Talent Ranking
data_sources.description: IMD ranking on talent investment, appeal, and readiness for business environment.
data_sources.submission_mechanism: Mix of hard data and executive surveys. Submitted via DEPS and IMD partner.

data_sources.name: WHO — Global Health Observatory
data_sources.description: World Health Organization statistics including life expectancy, health expenditure, and disease burden.
data_sources.submission_mechanism: Data submitted by Ministry of Health annually via WHO Country Health Information reporting system.
data_sources.brunei_contact: Ministry of Health — Health Information Unit

data_sources.name: World Bank — World Development Indicators
data_sources.description: Comprehensive cross-country statistics on economic development, trade, and social outcomes.
data_sources.submission_mechanism: Drawn from national statistical reporting pipelines. DEPS is primary submission point. Requires consistent GDP, trade, and population data reporting.
data_sources.brunei_contact: DEPS / Ministry of Finance and Economy

data_sources.name: World Justice Project — Rule of Law Index
data_sources.description: Annual assessment of rule of law across 8 factors including constraints on government, fundamental rights, and civil justice.
data_sources.submission_mechanism: Household and expert surveys conducted in-country. Brunei can request expanded country inclusion or improve survey respondent diversity.

data_sources.name: Economist Intelligence Unit
data_sources.description: EIU Democracy Index and Business Environment assessments.
data_sources.submission_mechanism: Expert-assessed by EIU analysts. Limited direct submission. Brunei can improve via policy reforms and engaging EIU country desk.

indicators.name: Government Effectiveness
indicators.definition: The government effectiveness indicator captures perceptions of the quality of public services, the quality of the civil service and the degree of its independence from political pressures, the quality of policy formulation and implementation, and the credibility of the government's commitment to such policies. Scores are standardised (2.5 = low; 2.5 = high).
indicators.source_name: World Bank, The Worldwide Governance Indicators
indicators.insead_methodology: World Bank aggregates perceptions from UN agencies, ILO, UNODC, expert surveys, media reports. Standardized index -2.5 to +2.5
indicators.brunei_comment: Brunei improving; government civil service reforms recognized
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Strengthen outcome-based performance management across ministries, expand competency-based civil service reforms, and accelerate digitalisation of frontline public services through one-stop e-government platforms.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Designate PMO/Public Service Department as focal point, compile an annual English governance reform brief with service-delivery results, and systematically share updated reforms and evidence with World Bank governance data partners before cut-off dates.
indicators.submission_notes: WGI composite derived from underlying sources including WEF EOS, IMD WCY, and others. Brunei should focus on improving business regulation quality scores in WEF and IMD surveys.
indicators.brunei_challenge: Regulatory environment perceived as overly bureaucratic; e-licensing improvements needed

indicators.name: Rule of Law
indicators.definition: The rule of law indicator reflects perceptions of the extent to which agents have confidence in and abide by the rules of society, and in particular the quality of contract enforcement, property rights, the police, and the courts, as well as the likelihood of crime and violence. Scores are standardised (2.5 = low; 2.5 = high).
indicators.insead_methodology: World Bank aggregates legal/justice indicators from multiple sources. -2.5 to +2.5 standardization reflects expert surveys and administrative data
indicators.brunei_comment: Strong improvement (+11.10) reflects strengthened judiciary/enforcement
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Enhance judicial capacity through continuous professional training and specialisation in commercial and cyber cases, expand alternative dispute resolution, and improve contract enforcement through time-bound case processing standards.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Regularly transmit justice sector statistics such as case clearance rates, backlog, and enforcement timelines to international data partners, maintain public online access to legislation and judgments, and verify that recent legal reforms are reflected in source datasets.
indicators.submission_notes: WGI composite. Improved judicial independence and contract enforcement perceptions would raise this score.
indicators.brunei_challenge: Perception gap in judicial independence and access to legal remedies

indicators.name: Political Stability
indicators.definition: The political stability and absence of violence indicator measures perceptions of the likelihood of political instability and/or politically motivated violence, including terrorism. Scores are standardised (2.5 = low; 2.5 = high).
indicators.insead_methodology: World Bank aggregates terrorism/conflict data, political risk indices, expert assessments. Standardized -2.5 to +2.5
indicators.brunei_comment: CRITICAL: Decline may reflect methodology change, not actual instability
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Maintain investment in community cohesion, strategic communications, and national resilience planning, while institutionalising whole-of-government scenario planning and crisis response coordination.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Engage the World Bank to clarify methodological shifts, provide contextual notes when isolated incidents may distort perceptions, and ensure security, crime, and disaster-response administrative data are submitted completely and on time through international reporting channels.
indicators.submission_notes: One of Brunei's strongest scores. Absolute monarchy and small geographic size contribute to perceived stability.
indicators.brunei_challenge: Maintain current standing

indicators.name: Regulatory Quality
indicators.definition: The regulatory quality indicator captures perceptions of the ability of the government to formulate and implement sound policies and regulations that permit and promote private sector development. Scores are standardised (2.5 = low; 2.5 = high).
indicators.insead_methodology: World Bank aggregates regulatory indices, expert surveys on business environment, policy consistency assessments. Standardized -2.5 to +2.5
indicators.brunei_comment: Stable performance indicates consistent regulatory environment
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Institutionalise Regulatory Impact Assessment for major regulations, strengthen structured consultation with SMEs and investors, and expand digital licensing and single-window services to reduce compliance burden.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Maintain a documented inventory of key regulatory reforms and implementation evidence, share annual updates with relevant international agencies, and brief business associations so survey respondents are aware of recent improvements.
indicators.submission_notes: Measures quality of public services, civil service, policy formulation. Digital government services expansion would improve this.
indicators.brunei_challenge: Public service delivery speed; e-government adoption lag

indicators.name: Corruption Perceptions Index
indicators.definition: The Corruption Perceptions Index aggregates data from a number of different sources that provide perceptions of business people and country experts of the level of corruption in the public sector.
indicators.insead_methodology: Transparency International aggregates 13 data sources: World Bank, Economist Intelligence Unit, African Development Bank, IMF, etc. Scale 0-100
indicators.brunei_comment: n/a - Not reported in 2025 GTCI for Brunei
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Strengthen anti-corruption enforcement capacity, whistleblower protection, and integrity risk management, and publish more anonymised data on investigations, sanctions, and preventive controls.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Nominate the Anti-Corruption Bureau as CPI focal point, compile annual evidence on laws, enforcement, and institutional reforms, and proactively engage data providers and expert communities to ensure Brunei's integrity efforts are fully understood.
indicators.verification_by_mpb:
Brunei continues to be a very strong performer in public sector transparency, comfortably exceeding both regional and global averages.
CPI Score: 63 / 100 (0 is highly corrupt; 100 is very clean)
Global Rank: 31st out of the total index.
Long-Term Trend: Transparency International classifies Brunei as a long-term "improver" in the Asia-Pacific region. Since 2012, Brunei has demonstrated significant and steady progress in its anti-corruption trajectory
indicators.submission_notes: WGI composite. ACB performance and public sector transparency are key drivers.
indicators.brunei_challenge: Perception of elite capture; limited independent oversight institutions

indicators.name: Extent of Market Dominance
indicators.definition: Average answer to the question: In your country, how do you characterise corporate activity? [1 = dominated by a few business groups; 7 = spread among many firms].
indicators.insead_methodology: WEF Executive Opinion Survey: 1,400+ respondents globally per year. Question: corporate activity dominated by few (1) vs spread many (7). Unweighted average
indicators.brunei_comment: Brunei score 39.42 indicates still concentrated market; improving
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Operationalise competition policy, reduce barriers for new entrants, and review sectors where market concentration remains high so that more firms can participate in contestable activities.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Work with the WEF survey partner to broaden the respondent pool beyond dominant incumbents to include SMEs and newer firms, and provide a short reform brief before survey administration.
indicators.submission_notes: Survey-based. Increase number of WEF EOS respondents and promote private sector sophistication through FDI attraction.
indicators.brunei_challenge: Small private sector; limited cluster development; low entrepreneurship scores

indicators.name: Domestic Credit to Private Sector
indicators.definition: Domestic credit to private sector refers to loans, purchases of nonequity securities, trade credits, and other financial resources that need to be repaid and that are provided to the private sector by financial corporations.
indicators.source_name: IMF International Financial Statistics, World Bank WDI
indicators.insead_methodology: World Bank collects from central banks/regulators: loans + non-equity securities + trade credits / GDP. Percentage of GDP
indicators.brunei_comment: CRITICAL: -20 point drop (35.42→15.42) indicates severe credit access constraints for businesses
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Expand SME credit guarantees and risk-sharing schemes, strengthen credit information infrastructure, and promote alternative financing channels such as venture funding and Islamic finance instruments for growth firms.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Ensure Brunei Darussalam Central Bank and MOFE reconcile definitions with IMF and World Bank methodology, validate sharp year-to-year breaks, and submit timely series disaggregated by borrower type and sector.
indicators.submission_notes: Survey-based. Darussalam Enterprise (DARe) programmes and new SME policies could improve executive perception.
indicators.brunei_challenge: Limited venture capital, high regulatory burden for new businesses, small market size

indicators.name: Cluster Development
indicators.definition: Average answer to the question: In your country, how widespread are well-developed and deep clusters (geographic concentrations of firms, suppliers, producers of related products and services, and specialised institutions in a particular field)? [1 = nonexistent; 7 = widespread in many fields].
indicators.insead_methodology: WEF EOS survey: 1-7 scale on geographic cluster concentration. Unweighted average of respondents
indicators.brunei_comment: Brunei 44.29 = limited industrial clusters; low diversification
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Develop focused cluster strategies in priority sectors such as downstream oil and gas, halal industries, digital services, and tourism, supported by shared infrastructure, supplier development, and university-industry collaboration.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Document existing cluster initiatives and anchor firms in English and share with WEF-related channels, and encourage cluster participants to join perception surveys so current ecosystem depth is recognised.
indicators.submission_notes: IMD WCY survey item. Labour market flexibility improvements and streamlined work permit processes would improve score.
indicators.brunei_challenge: Complex work permit process; quota systems; perceived inflexibility

indicators.name: R&D Expenditure
indicators.definition: Gross expenditure on R&D (GERD) is the total domestic intramural expenditure on R&D during a given period as a percentage of GDP. Intramural R&D expenditure is all expenditure for R&D performed within a statistical unit or sector of the economy during a specific period, regardless of the source of funding.
indicators.source_name: UNESCO Institute for Statistics, WIPO GII, OECD MSTI
indicators.insead_methodology: UNESCO/OECD/NSF collect gross expenditure on R&D (GERD) from government/business/academia. % of GDP
indicators.brunei_comment: Brunei 4.29% = VERY LOW. Developed countries 2-3%+. Must increase R&D investment
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Create a medium-term national R&D funding framework, introduce matching grants or tax incentives for private R&D, and strengthen mission-oriented research partnerships between government, universities, and industry.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Establish a single national R&D statistics system covering government, higher education, and business using Frascati-aligned definitions, and report consolidated GERD data with metadata to UNESCO, OECD, and WIPO.
indicators.verification_by_mpb: could be a composite data. requires another pair of eyes to confirm and im suspecting INSEAD are using multiple sources of information
indicators.submission_notes: Survey. Business regulatory reform (e.g. reducing permit requirements, ICTF simplification) would improve this score.
indicators.brunei_challenge: Complex permit processes; slow bureaucracy; multiple ministry touchpoints for business

indicators.name: Population covered by at least a 3G Mobile Network
indicators.definition: Percentage of the population covered by at least a 3G mobile-cellular network signal; values are calculated by dividing the number of inhabitants covered by at least a 3G mobile-cellular signal by the total population and multiplying by 100.
indicators.insead_methodology: ITU collects from telecom regulators: % population covered by 3G network (new 2025 indicator)
indicators.brunei_comment: Brunei 93.09 = excellent mobile infrastructure. Competitive advantage
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Maintain near-universal mobile coverage while upgrading lagging areas through infrastructure sharing, targeted obligations in spectrum awards, and continued investment in resilient mobile networks.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Coordinate AITI and operators to validate population coverage maps, submit harmonised methodology notes to ITU DataHub, and routinely cross-check international values against national network planning data.
indicators.verification_by_mpb:
Brunei Darussalam Performance (At least 3G)
Brunei has shown steady, incremental growth in its 3G population coverage over the recorded years, closing the gap toward full coverage:

2018–2021: Coverage hovered between 95.9% and 96.6%.

2022: Saw a significant jump up to 98.6%.

2023–2024: Reached and stabilized at its highest recorded peak of 99.1%.

🇸🇬 Singapore Comparison
2018–2024: Singapore has maintained a flawless 100% population coverage for at least 3G across every single year on record.
indicators.submission_notes: NA
indicators.brunei_challenge: NA

indicators.name: Internet access in schools
indicators.definition: Proportion of primary schools with access to Internet for pedagogical purposes; Internet access includes fixed narrowband, fixed broadband, or mobile networks used to enhance teaching and learning.
indicators.insead_methodology: UNESCO Institute for Statistics, UIS.Stat
indicators.brunei_comment: n/a - Not reported in 2025 GTCI for Brunei
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Complete universal school connectivity by setting minimum bandwidth standards, upgrading internal school networks, and linking connectivity to digital teaching and learning programmes.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Add standardised connectivity variables to EMIS, validate them annually through school audits, and report the series consistently to UNESCO and other education data systems so Brunei is no longer missing.
indicators.verification_by_mpb: No data was uploaded to UIS website
indicators.submission_notes: NA
indicators.brunei_challenge: NA

indicators.name: Urbanisation
indicators.definition: Population of urban areas as a percentage of total population; urban population refers to people living in urban areas as defined by national statistical offices.
indicators.submission_agency: Department of Economic Planning & Statistics - Population Census
indicators.insead_methodology: United Nations Population Division
indicators.brunei_comment: Brunei 75.76. rank remained but score decreased from 74.28 (2023)
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Align urbanisation policy with economic diversification through well-serviced growth centres, better public transport, and compact urban planning that supports jobs and quality of life.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Ensure Brunei's definition of urban areas is fully documented and communicated to the UN Population Division, and that census and register updates are reflected promptly in international datasets.
indicators.submission_notes: NA
indicators.brunei_challenge: NA

indicators.name: Labour Rights
indicators.definition: Level of national compliance with fundamental labour rights (freedom of association and collective bargaining). It ranges from 0 (best score, higher compliance) to 10 (worst score, lower compliance) based on ILO supervisory sources and national legislation.
indicators.submission_agency: Prime Minister's Office/ Ministry of Home Affairs
indicators.insead_methodology: ILO measures compliance with freedom of association/collective bargaining based on national legislation + supervisory body assessments. 0-10 scale
indicators.brunei_comment: Brunei 74.20 = good labour rights compliance
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Continue aligning labour legislation with ILO core principles, strengthen labour inspection and dispute resolution, and institutionalise structured social dialogue between government, employers, and workers.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Submit regular legislative and enforcement updates to ILO reporting channels, and ensure Brunei's compliance measures and reforms are clearly reflected in ILO metadata and supervisory documentation.
indicators.verification_by_mpb:
Over the last five years, Brunei Darussalam has maintained a completely unchanged score for its level of national compliance with labour rights.

2024 Value: 2.6

2023 Value: 2.6

2022 Value: 2.6

2021 Value: 2.6

2020 Value: 2.6
indicators.submission_notes: Hard data submitted to ITU annually. Brunei must increase fixed broadband penetration — current gap vs SG is -26 points.
indicators.brunei_challenge: High mobile internet use but low fixed broadband penetration; infrastructure cost in rural areas

indicators.name: Labour-Employer Cooperation
indicators.definition: Average answer to the question: In your country, how do you characterise labour-employer relations? [1 = generally confrontational; 7 = generally cooperative].
indicators.insead_methodology: WEF EOS survey: 1-7 scale on confrontational vs cooperative labour relations. Unweighted average
indicators.brunei_comment: Brunei 60.53 = moderate cooperation; improved +9.33
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Build stronger bipartite and tripartite cooperation platforms, promote joint productivity and training initiatives, and encourage company-level problem-solving mechanisms between employers and workers.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Prepare concise examples of cooperative labour relations and share them with relevant WEF survey networks, while encouraging informed employer and HR respondents to participate.
indicators.submission_notes: Strong performance. Brunei's 5G rollout is underway. Annual ITU reporting through AITI.
indicators.brunei_challenge: Maintain and expand 5G rollout; ensure rural coverage parity

indicators.name: Professional Management
indicators.definition: Average answer to the question: In your country, who holds senior management positions in companies? [1 = usually relatives or friends without regard to merit; 7 = mostly professional managers chosen for merit and qualifications].
indicators.submission_agency: Department of Economic Planning & Statistics - Economic Census of Enterprises
indicators.insead_methodology: WEF EOS survey: 1-7 scale on senior management selection (relatives vs merit-based). Unweighted average of respondents
indicators.brunei_comment: Brunei +18.19 improvement = major shift toward merit-based hiring (42.44→60.63)
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Promote merit-based recruitment and succession planning in SOEs and larger firms, and expand leadership and management development programmes tied to competency frameworks.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Document governance and HR reforms in public communications and employer materials, and engage chambers and professional bodies so WEF respondents recognise the shift toward professional management.
indicators.submission_notes: Composite access score. Improving fixed broadband and household computer access would boost this.
indicators.brunei_challenge: Device affordability and digital skills limiting full ICT access

indicators.name: Relationship of Pay-Productivity
indicators.definition: Average answer to the question: In your country, to what extent does average pay reflect productivity? [1 = not at all; 7 = to a great extent].
indicators.insead_methodology: WEF EOS survey: 1-7 scale on extent pay reflects productivity. Unweighted average
indicators.brunei_comment: Brunei 46.51 DECLINING (-8.66) = pay increasingly divorced from performance. HR concern
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Promote productivity-linked pay systems, firm-level performance management, and sector productivity diagnostics to better align wages with measurable output and value creation.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Collect and publish examples of productivity-based remuneration schemes, and strengthen dissemination of wage and productivity statistics so external experts can see evidence of reform.
indicators.submission_notes: Index assessed by UNDESA. Brunei must accelerate digital government services, open data portals, and citizen digital participation.
indicators.brunei_challenge: Limited open government data; services not fully digital; e-participation low

indicators.name: Enterprise Software
indicators.definition: Enterprise software market refers to the size of the market for software used for essential large-scale business activities in professional B2B environments (including ERP, CRM, BI, and SCM), weighted by national GDP.
indicators.source_name: Statista Technology Market Outlook, World Bank WDI
indicators.insead_methodology: Statista tech market data: size of enterprise software market (ERP/CRM/BI/SCM) weighted by GDP
indicators.brunei_comment: Brunei 11.86 = minimal enterprise software adoption. Digital transformation gap
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Provide grants, tax deductions, or shared services to accelerate ERP, CRM, accounting, and business intelligence adoption among SMEs and public-linked enterprises.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Add enterprise software usage modules to national business ICT surveys, and share anonymised market evidence with international market data providers to improve country estimates.
indicators.verification_by_mpb: to view Statista website requires paid account (professional account).
indicators.submission_notes: NA
indicators.brunei_challenge: NA

indicators.name: Cloud Computing
indicators.definition: Cloud computing market refers to the size of the market for Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS), Platform as a Service (PaaS), and Software as a Service (SaaS), weighted by national GDP.
indicators.source_name: Statista Technology Market Outlook, World Bank WDI
indicators.insead_methodology: Statista tech market data: cloud market (IaaS/PaaS/SaaS) weighted by GDP
indicators.brunei_comment: Brunei 4.79 = MINIMAL cloud adoption. Digital infrastructure gap
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Adopt a national cloud-first approach for government and larger enterprises, clarify regulatory treatment of cloud services, and support SMEs with secure migration and cyber readiness assistance.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Integrate cloud adoption questions into business ICT surveys, triangulate with operator and data centre statistics, and engage data providers to ensure Brunei's market size is not underestimated.
indicators.verification_by_mpb: to view Statista website requires paid account (professional account).
indicators.submission_notes: NA
indicators.brunei_challenge: NA

indicators.name: Firms with website
indicators.definition: Firms with website refers to the share of businesses that have their own website, expressed as a percentage of all firms.
indicators.csv_ministry_full: Ministry of Finance and Economy / Ministry of Transport and Infocommunications
indicators.insead_methodology: World Bank Enterprise Surveys: Percentage of firms possessing their own website.
indicators.brunei_comment: n/a - Not reported in 2025 GTCI for Brunei
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Support SMEs to establish websites and digital storefronts through subsidised onboarding, practical training, and integration with e-commerce and digital payments.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Include website ownership in enterprise and ICT surveys, and work with World Bank Enterprise Surveys or equivalent sources so Brunei has a robust, internationally comparable data point.
indicators.verification_by_mpb: In 2025, 84.9% (specifically 84.86%) of all surveyed firms in Brunei Darussalam had their own website.
indicators.submission_notes: NA
indicators.brunei_challenge: NA

indicators.name: FDI Regulatory Restrictiveness
indicators.definition: The Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) Regulatory Restrictiveness Index measure is a composite indicator developed by the OECD that provides a measure of the openness of FDI rules and regulations towards overseas investors. Four types of statutory restrictions are considered in the computation of the index: (1) foreign equity restrictions, (2) screening and prior approval requirements, (3) rules for key personnel, and (4) other restrictions on the operation of foreign enterprises. The index is measured on a scale of 0 (no restrictions) to 1 (completely restricted).
indicators.insead_methodology: OECD developed composite index measuring 4 restrictions: (1) equity limits, (2) screening/approval, (3) key personnel rules, (4) operation restrictions. 0=open, 1=closed
indicators.brunei_comment: Brunei 54.68 declining = MORE restrictive FDI environment in 2025. Concern for investment

indicators.verification_by_mpb:
Brunei's FDI Restrictiveness Score
Latest Score (2023): Brunei Darussalam received a score of approximately 0.187 (as visualized in the chart ranging around 0.1875).

Trend: The data indicates that this score has remained stable at this level between 2018 and 2023.

How the OECD Scoring Scale Works
The OECD FDI Regulatory Restrictiveness Index gauges how restrictive a country's foreign direct investment rules are based on a specific framework:

The Scale: The index is measured on a scale from 0 to 1.

0 represents a market that is fully open to FDI.
1 represents a market that is fully closed to FDI.
With a score of ~0.187, Brunei sits on the lower end of the scale, indicating a relatively open investment environment with some regulatory restrictions.
What it Measures: The final score is a combination of statutory restrictions across four main categories:
Foreign equity limits.
Discriminatory screening or approval mechanisms.
Restrictions on key foreign personnel.
Operational restrictions.

What it Excludes: The score strictly reflects formal, statutory measures that discriminate against foreign investors. It does not account for general investment climate factors like regulatory transparency, state monopolies, implementation issues, or preferential treatment within special economic zones.

indicators.name: Financial Globalization
indicators.definition: The Financial Globalisation (de facto) sub-index is one of the dimensions of the KOF Globalisation Index. It provides a measure of the degree of a country's integration in global financial flows. It is made up of five variables: (1) foreign direct investment, (2) portfolio investment, (3) international debt, (4) international reserves, and (5) international income payments. The sub-index is calculated by aggregating the five (normalised) variables, where each variable has been given a specific weight based on principal components analysis (PCA).

indicators.name: Migrant Stock
indicators.definition: Migrant stock refers to the number of the migrant stock population as a percentage of the total population. In producing the 2024 edition of the International Migrant Stock dataset, the Population Division has prioritised revising the estimates for countries with new empirical information from population censuses or registers and relatively large numbers of international migrants, as well as for countries affected by ongoing or emergent refugee flows as documented by UNHCR.

indicators.verification_by_mpb:
Based on the latest UN DESA International Migrant Stock (2024 Edition), Brunei’s demographic composition reflects a highly stable, structurally integrated migrant population.
Total Migrant Stock (2024): 119,933 individuals.
Proportion of Total Population (2024): 25.9% (approximately 1 in every 4 residents).
Trend Over Time: The scores have remained exceptionally stable, moving marginally from 25.2% in 2020 to 25.9% in 2024.

Significance of the Scores
1. High Structural Labor RelianceBrunei's migrant stock score of 25.9% is seven times higher than the global average of 3.7%. This extreme density highlights a foundational reliance on foreign labor to support the local economy. Migrant workers constitute roughly 24.5% of the total domestic workforce.
2. Resource-Driven Industry DynamicsUnlike diversified corporate hubs, Brunei's migrant intake is historically concentrated in the oil and gas industry, downstream petrochemical processing, and infrastructure construction. A significant secondary portion consists of service-sector workers and domestic helpers.
3. Balancing "Brunei-isation" PoliciesThe relative flatlining of the score (hovering around 25%) reflects strict regulatory controls. 

The government actively enforces "Brunei-isation" directives, which mandate quotas prioritizing the employment of local citizens. 
The data shows that the country maintains a strict equilibrium—bringing in necessary foreign expertise without inflating the long-term non-resident population.
4. High Protection Against Refugee Pressures 
Because the UN 2024 revision prioritizes adjustments for geopolitical refugee flows, Brunei's stable numbers confirm its isolation from these specific global crises. Its migrant stock is almost purely economic and employment-driven, dictated by state-issued work permits rather than humanitarian migration corridors.

indicators.name: Tolerance of minorities

indicators.name: Women Economic Empowerment

The index is measured on a scale of 0 (women have no legal economic rights equal to those of men) to 100 (women and men have equal economic opportunities).

indicators.insead_methodology: LinkedIn: Digital footprint/penetration of professional networks, measured as LinkedIn users per 1,000 labor force.
indicators.brunei_comment: Brunei score 36.47 (Rank 35). Good professional networking penetration.
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Encourage graduates and professionals to maintain active professional network profiles and use them for recruitment, collaboration, and skills signalling.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Engage LinkedIn or similar platforms for country-level audience validation, and encourage broader adoption so the indicator better reflects professional networking penetration.
indicators.submission_notes: NA
indicators.brunei_challenge: NA

indicators.name: Pension Coverage
indicators.definition: Pension coverage refers to the share of people above statutory retirement age who receive an old-age pension, expressed as a percentage of the population above statutory retirement age.
indicators.csv_ministry_full: Ministry of Finance and Economy / Tabung Amanah Pekerja / Ministry of Culture, Youth and Sports
indicators.source_name: International Labour Organization Social Security Inquiry
indicators.insead_methodology: ILO: % population above statutory retirement age receiving old-age pension. ILO Social Security Inquiry data
indicators.brunei_comment: Brunei 100.00 = WORLD RANK 1 = universal pension coverage. Excellent retention support
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Maintain universal pension coverage while strengthening long-term sustainability through periodic actuarial review, strong fund governance, and complementary retirement savings options.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Keep pension coverage data current in ILO and SDG reporting channels and document scheme design clearly so universal coverage continues to be recognised.
indicators.submission_notes: Hard data. Brunei's labour force occupational classification data must be submitted to ILO annually. TVET expansion would shift workforce composition.
indicators.brunei_challenge: Dependence on low-skilled foreign labour; insufficient mid-level skill development

indicators.name: Social Protection
indicators.definition: Average answer to the question: In your country, to what extent do all members of the population have sufficient access to safety nets and social services in terms of unemployment income, re-training and re-employment support? [1 = not at all – service is difficult or expensive to access for all; 7 = to a great extent – service is easy and affordable to access for all].
indicators.csv_ministry_full: Ministry of Finance and Economy / Tabung Amanah Pekerja / Ministry of Culture, Youth and Sports
indicators.insead_methodology: WEF EOS survey: 1-7 scale on access to safety nets/social services (unemployment, retraining support). Unweighted average
indicators.brunei_comment: Brunei 50.00 = declined (-5.97). Safety net concerns need addressing
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Improve access to unemployment support, retraining, and targeted assistance for vulnerable groups, and make social services easier to navigate through integrated delivery channels.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Document the full architecture of Brunei's safety nets in English and transmit administrative coverage data consistently so international assessors capture the breadth of support available.
indicators.submission_notes: Hard data. Brunei scores zero — data is either not submitted or R&D technician headcount is genuinely near zero. Urgent data collection and submission needed.
indicators.brunei_challenge: Zero score = either data gap or structural absence of R&D technician roles

indicators.name: Brain Retention
indicators.definition: Average answer to the question: To what extent does your country attract/retain talented people? [1 = not at all; 7 = to a great extent].
indicators.insead_methodology: WEF EOS survey: 1-7 scale on country ability to attract/retain talented people. Unweighted average
indicators.brunei_comment: Brunei 48.17 = improved (+5.43). Better retention of talent (rank 61)
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Strengthen retention of high-skilled nationals through competitive career pathways, research opportunities, and returnee talent schemes linked to strategic sectors.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Prepare concise evidence on retention and return initiatives and share it with survey intermediaries, while engaging employers who have successfully retained talent.
indicators.submission_notes: NA
indicators.brunei_challenge: NA

indicators.name: Environmental Performance
indicators.definition: The Environmental Performance Index (EPI) ranks how well countries perform in two fundamental dimensions of sustainable development: environmental health and ecosystem vitality, using 32 indicators grouped into eleven issue areas. Indicators measure proximity to internationally established targets or, in their absence, performance relative to best-performing countries.
indicators.source_name: Yale Center for Environmental Law & Policy EPI 2024
indicators.submission_agency: Department of Environment, Parks and Recreation
indicators.insead_methodology: Yale Environmental Performance Index: 32 indicators across environmental health and ecosystem vitality. 0-100 scale
indicators.brunei_comment: Brunei 48.30 = improving (+2.88). Environmental sustainability efforts working
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Accelerate priority actions under environmental and climate policies, especially in biodiversity, pollution control, and resilience, with measurable implementation targets.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Strengthen environmental statistics systems and submit complete, up-to-date data to international repositories used in the Environmental Performance Index, with clear metadata for any estimated values.
indicators.submission_notes: NA
indicators.brunei_challenge: NA

indicators.name: Vulnerable Employment
indicators.definition: Vulnerable employment refers to the number of contributing family workers and own-account workers, expressed as a percentage of total employment. Own-account workers are self-employed workers without employees; contributing family workers are unpaid family workers.
indicators.insead_methodology: ILO: % of employment in vulnerable forms (own-account, unpaid family). Lower = better. Based on labour surveys
indicators.brunei_comment: Brunei 95.02 = RANK 10 = very LOW vulnerable employment. Excellent job security
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Sustain low vulnerable employment through continued formal job creation, SME formalisation support, and enforcement of labour standards for more precarious work arrangements.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Ensure labour force classification follows ILO guidance exactly and provide regular, disaggregated updates to ILOSTAT to preserve Brunei's strong performance.
indicators.submission_notes: NA
indicators.brunei_challenge: NA

indicators.name: Protect against future disasters
indicators.definition: Percentage of respondents who answered "yes" to the question about whether they could protect themselves or their family in a future disaster, based on the World Risk Poll.
indicators.insead_methodology: Gallup World Poll / Lloyd's Register Foundation: Perception of being protected against future disasters.
indicators.brunei_comment: n/a - Not reported in 2025 GTCI for Brunei
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Expand community disaster preparedness, resilience education, and protective infrastructure so households feel more able to cope with future shocks.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Seek stronger Brunei representation in World Risk Poll-type surveys and complement international polling with national preparedness perception surveys that can be shared externally.
indicators.submission_notes: NA
indicators.brunei_challenge: NA

indicators.name: Household financial resilience
indicators.definition: Percentage of respondents who reported that their household could survive for four months or more if income was lost, in response to the question on how long the household could cover basic needs if income was lost.
indicators.submission_agency: Department of Economic Planning & Statistics - Household Expenditure Survey
indicators.insead_methodology: World Bank Findex: Percentage of respondents who indicate they could come up with emergency funds if needed.
indicators.brunei_comment: n/a - Not reported in 2025 GTCI for Brunei
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Promote savings, takaful/insurance, and household financial planning through financial literacy and inclusive financial products that improve shock resilience.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Integrate financial resilience questions into household and financial inclusion surveys, and work toward inclusion in international datasets such as Global Findex updates.
indicators.submission_notes: NA
indicators.brunei_challenge: NA

indicators.name: Personal rights
indicators.definition: Personal Rights are a component of the Opportunity dimension of the Social Progress Index, based on six variables: access to justice, freedom of religion, political rights, property rights for women, freedom of peaceful assembly, and freedom of discussion.
indicators.insead_methodology: Social Progress Index: Composite index measuring political rights, freedom of expression, and freedom of assembly.
indicators.brunei_comment: n/a - Not reported in 2025 GTCI for Brunei
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Continue strengthening access to justice, administrative fairness, and feedback channels so personal rights protections are more visible and better understood externally.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Provide Social Progress Index-related data partners with clear legal and institutional documentation on rights protections, complaint mechanisms, and recent reforms.
indicators.submission_notes: Survey. Improving TVET-industry linkages and publishing graduate employability statistics would help.
indicators.brunei_challenge: Skills mismatch between education output and labour market demand

indicators.name: Personal safety
indicators.definition: Personal safety is a component of the Basic Human Needs dimension of the Social Progress Index, based on indicators of interpersonal violence, transportation-related fatalities, political killings and torture, intimate partner violence, and money stolen.
indicators.insead_methodology: Social Progress Index: Composite index measuring homicide rate, violent crime, perceived criminality, and traffic deaths.
indicators.brunei_comment: n/a - Not reported in 2025 GTCI for Brunei
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Maintain strong safety outcomes through targeted crime prevention, road safety enforcement, and community policing, while responding quickly to emerging risks.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Ensure complete and timely reporting of crime, safety, and transport fatality statistics to international repositories that feed composite indicators.
indicators.submission_notes: Hard data. Brunei's youth unemployment is structurally high (~22%). Data submitted to ILO via DEPS. Urgent jobs creation and skills matching needed.
indicators.brunei_challenge: Structural youth unemployment; over-dependence on public sector jobs; skills mismatch

indicators.name: Physician density
indicators.definition: Physician density refers to the number of medical doctors (physicians), including generalist and specialist medical practitioners, per 10,000 people.
indicators.insead_methodology: WHO: Medical doctors/physicians per 10,000 population.
indicators.brunei_comment: Brunei score 27.37 (Rank 77). Moderate physician density.
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Expand physician supply through domestic medical training, scholarships, and targeted recruitment in shortage specialties, while improving retention of medical professionals.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Update WHO workforce statistics regularly using current physician counts and verified population estimates, and ensure classifications match WHO reporting standards.
indicators.submission_notes: Hard data via World Bank. GDP and employment data must be current. Economic diversification drives productivity gains.
indicators.brunei_challenge: O&G sector skews productivity figure; non-oil economy has lower productivity

indicators.name: Sanitation
indicators.definition: Population using at least basic sanitation services (%): the percentage of the population using improved sanitation facilities that are not shared with other households, including those using safely managed sanitation services.
indicators.insead_methodology: WHO/UNICEF JMP: Percentage of population using at least basic sanitation services.
indicators.brunei_comment: Brunei score 98.00 (Rank 43). Excellent basic sanitation infrastructure.
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Sustain high sanitation coverage and continue upgrading toward safely managed services, wastewater treatment quality, and resilience of sanitation infrastructure.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Maintain high-quality JMP reporting with disaggregation by service ladder and validate that service improvements beyond basic access are captured in international databases.
indicators.submission_notes: NA
indicators.brunei_challenge: NA

indicators.name: Employee Wellbeing
indicators.definition: Happiness score refers to the national average response to the Cantril life ladder question, where respondents rate their current life on a scale from 0 (worst possible life) to 10 (best possible life).
indicators.csv_ministry_full: Ministry of Health / Ministry of Culture, Youth and Sports
indicators.insead_methodology: Gallup World Poll: Percentage of respondents who feel their workplace supports their employee wellbeing.
indicators.brunei_comment: n/a - Not reported in 2025 GTCI for Brunei
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Encourage employers to adopt wellbeing and mental health programmes, flexible work arrangements, and healthier workplace standards supported by national guidance.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Participate in or commission credible employee wellbeing surveys and share externally visible results where compatible with Gallup-style measures.
indicators.submission_notes: NA
indicators.brunei_challenge: NA

indicators.name: Workforce Secondary Education
indicators.definition: Workforce with secondary education refers to the percentage of the labour force (above 15 years old) whose highest educational attainment is at the secondary level, based on ISCED 2011 levels 3–4.
indicators.insead_methodology: ILO: % of employed population with at least secondary education. Based on labour force surveys + census. ISCO occupation mapping
indicators.brunei_comment: Brunei MAJOR -10.60 decline (73.23→62.63) = workforce education levels declining. CONCERN
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Expand second-chance and adult learning pathways for workers without completed secondary education, including modular qualifications and recognition of prior learning.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Review educational attainment coding in labour force surveys, validate time-series consistency with ILOSTAT, and explain any methodological changes behind recent declines.
indicators.submission_notes: Hard data. High-skilled worker share reflects education outcomes and labour market structure.
indicators.brunei_challenge: Brain drain of high-skilled Bruneians; insufficient high-skill local job opportunities

indicators.name: Population Secondary Education
indicators.definition: Population with secondary education refers to the percentage of the population (above 25 years old) whose highest educational attainment is at the secondary level, based on ISCED 2011 levels 3–4.
indicators.insead_methodology: ILO: % of total population (age 15+) with secondary education. New separate indicator in 2025
indicators.brunei_comment: Brunei 63.94% = 27th globally. Good but not excellent secondary completion
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Increase overall secondary attainment through adult completion programmes, community outreach, and targeted support for older or vulnerable cohorts.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Provide harmonised attainment series for the adult population to international agencies and document reclassification issues or cohort effects that affect comparability.
indicators.verification_by_mpb: only Brunei has the data for 2023 (latest), not even Malaysia and Singapore have the data
indicators.submission_notes: Survey. Pedagogical reforms in SPN21 to promote inquiry-based learning, Socratic method, and critical thinking are needed.
indicators.brunei_challenge: Rote learning culture persists; teacher training lacks critical thinking methodology

indicators.name: Technicians & Associate Professionals
indicators.definition: Technicians and associate professionals refers to the number of technicians and associate professionals as a share of the total workforce, based on ISCO Revision 2008 (or Rev. 1988 where appropriate).
indicators.insead_methodology: ILO: % workforce in ISCO-08 Major Group 3 (technicians/associate professionals). Based on labour force surveys
indicators.brunei_comment: Brunei +6.94 improvement (50.12→57.06) = more mid-level technical jobs (rank 36)
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Grow technician and associate professional roles by aligning TVET and tertiary technical programmes with industry demand and establishing clear occupational pipelines.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Ensure labour force survey occupations are coded to ISCO-08 in sufficient detail and share updated occupational distributions with international databases.
indicators.submission_notes: Composite from standardised test performance. PISA scores are key inputs. Curriculum reform is essential.
indicators.brunei_challenge: Below-average PISA performance; lower cognitive skill outcomes vs income peers

indicators.name: Labour Productivity per Employee
indicators.definition: Labour productivity per person employed is obtained by dividing total output (GDP in constant PPP US dollars) by total labour input used (labour force) to produce that output.
indicators.source_name: The Conference Board, Total Economy Database
indicators.insead_methodology: Conference Board: Labour productivity measured as output per worker (GDP constant 2017 PPP $ per person employed).
indicators.brunei_comment: n/a - Not reported in 2025 GTCI for Brunei
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Raise labour productivity through sector upgrading, SME digitalisation, stronger management practices, and innovation support in higher value-added activities.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Coordinate GDP and employment data used by international productivity databases, and reconcile any revisions so Brunei's productivity estimates are not understated.
indicators.submission_notes: NA
indicators.brunei_challenge: NA

indicators.name: Finding Skilled Employees
indicators.definition: Average answer to the question: In your country, to what extent can companies find people with the skills required to fill their vacancies in the local labour market? [1 = not at all; 7 = to a great extent].
indicators.insead_methodology: WEF EOS survey: 1-7 scale on difficulty finding skilled workers. Unweighted average. HIGHER score = MORE difficult
indicators.brunei_comment: Brunei 42.88 = RANK 119 = WORLD'S WORST difficulty. CRITICAL SKILLS SHORTAGE
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Use national manpower planning to target shortage occupations, expand relevant training pipelines, and combine local workforce development with strategic foreign recruitment where necessary.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Communicate sector-specific skills improvements clearly to WEF-linked respondent networks and ensure knowledgeable employers participate in survey rounds.
indicators.submission_notes: Survey. Cross-cutting indicator requiring coordinated national talent strategy.
indicators.brunei_challenge: No national talent attraction strategy; fragmented ministry coordination

indicators.name: Education System Relevance
indicators.definition: Average answer to the question: In your country, how well does the education system meet the needs of a competitive economy? [1 = not at all; 7 = extremely well].
indicators.insead_methodology: WEF EOS survey: 1-7 scale on education system relevance to labour market economy. Unweighted average
indicators.brunei_comment: Brunei 60.78 declining (-3.18) = education-economy mismatch growing (rank 26)
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Strengthen employer involvement in curriculum design, internships, apprenticeships, and graduate tracer feedback to make education more relevant to labour-market needs.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Publish regular tracer studies and employer satisfaction findings in English and circulate them to international stakeholders who shape perception-based indicators.
indicators.submission_notes: Survey. AITI's Digital Skills Programme and STEM education expansion would directly improve this score.
indicators.brunei_challenge: Digital literacy levels below regional peers; coding education underrepresented in schools

indicators.name: Skills Matching
indicators.definition: Skills matching refers to the percentage of the workforce whose education matches their occupation, measured using a normative approach based on a correspondence table between occupations and required education levels (ISCED 2011 and ISCO).
indicators.insead_methodology: WEF EOS survey: 1-7 scale on extent of skills-education matching in labour market. Unweighted average
indicators.brunei_comment: Brunei 65.70 = moderate matching but declining (-1.11). Skills-job mismatch issue
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Improve career guidance, labour market information, and reskilling pathways so workers and graduates move into roles better matched to their qualifications and capabilities.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Link education and labour market datasets more effectively to measure mismatch, and publish clearer explanatory notes and evidence on matching outcomes.
indicators.submission_notes: NA
indicators.brunei_challenge: NA

indicators.name: Educated Unemployment
indicators.definition: Highly educated unemployment refers to the unemployment rate among the labour force with intermediate or advanced education, including upper secondary, post-secondary non-tertiary, short-cycle tertiary, bachelor's, master's and doctoral levels as defined by ISCED 2011.
indicators.insead_methodology: ILO: reverse metric = (1 - highly educated unemployment rate). Higher = lower unemployment of tertiary-educated. Based on labour surveys
indicators.brunei_comment: Brunei 83.21 = rank 48 = low unemployment of educated. Good job market for graduates
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Sustain low educated unemployment through tighter enrolment planning, stronger graduate employability support, and diversified pathways into entrepreneurship and regional opportunities.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Continue reporting unemployment by education level using internationally comparable definitions, and document any survey redesigns that may affect trend interpretation.
indicators.submission_notes: NA
indicators.brunei_challenge: NA

indicators.name: Workforce with Tertiary Education
indicators.definition: Labour force with tertiary education refers to the percentage of the labour force whose highest educational attainment is at the tertiary level, based on ISCED 2011 levels 5–8.
indicators.insead_methodology: ILO: % of workforce with tertiary education (ISCED 2011 levels 5-8). Based on labour force surveys
indicators.brunei_comment: Brunei +7.86 improvement (27.70→35.56) = more university graduates in workforce
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Increase tertiary-qualified workforce share through expanded flexible higher education, upskilling for adults, and attraction or return of Bruneian graduates from abroad.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Ensure labour force surveys fully capture recognised tertiary qualifications aligned to ISCED 5-8 and share updated series with international agencies.
indicators.submission_notes: Survey. Labour law modernisation and removal of fixed-wage determination constraints needed.
indicators.brunei_challenge: Rigid public sector salary scales; limited performance-based pay in private sector

indicators.name: Soft Skills
indicators.definition: Average answer to the question: In your country, to what extent is the workforce proficient in the following skills? [1 = not at all; 7 = to a great extent]: creativity and problem-solving; management skills; self-efficacy; working with others.
indicators.insead_methodology: WEF EOS survey: 1-7 scale on workforce soft skills (problem-solving, creativity, teamwork, communication). NEW indicator 2025
indicators.brunei_comment: Brunei 57.13 = mid-range soft skills (rank 60). Room for improvement
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Embed communication, teamwork, leadership, and problem-solving in curricula, training programmes, and workplace development initiatives across sectors.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Document soft-skills initiatives and employer outcomes in English and ensure informed respondents can reflect these improvements in survey-based indicators.
indicators.submission_notes: Hard data. Brunei's life expectancy (~76 years) is moderate. Non-communicable disease prevention and healthy lifestyle initiatives are key.
indicators.brunei_challenge: High NCD burden (diabetes, hypertension); obesity rates among highest in SEA

indicators.name: Professionals
indicators.definition: Professionals (%) refers to the share of total employment accounted for by professionals, including physical, mathematical and engineering science professionals; life science and health professionals; teaching professionals; and other professionals (business, legal, archivists, librarians, social science, religious professionals, and writers and creative or performing artists).
indicators.source_name:
International Labour Organization ILOSTAT
World Bank, Global Jobs Indicators Database (JOIN)
indicators.insead_methodology: ILO: % workforce in professional occupations (ISCO-08 Major Group 2: scientists, engineers, ICT specialists). Based on labour surveys
indicators.brunei_comment: Brunei 33.81 = slight decline (-0.70). Professional share stable (rank 45)
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Promote growth of professional occupations in high-priority sectors through scholarships, licensing pathways, and attractive advancement opportunities.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Strengthen occupational reporting and transmit detailed counts of professional employment to international statistical systems used by GTCI.
indicators.submission_notes: Hard data. Submitted to WHO via annual National Health Accounts. Increased health budget allocation would improve this.
indicators.brunei_challenge: Relatively low health expenditure; primary care underinvestment vs hospital spending

indicators.name: Researchers, Full-time equivalent
indicators.definition: Researchers in R&D are professionals engaged in the conception or creation of new knowledge, who conduct research and improve or develop concepts, theories, models, techniques, instrumentation, software or operational methods. The indicator is measured as researchers (full-time equivalent) per million population.
indicators.source_name:
Global Innovation Index Databas, WIPO 2025.
UNESCO Institute for Statistics (UIS)
OECD, Main Science and Technology Indicators (MSTI) Database
Ibero-American and Inter-American Network of Science and Technology Indicators (RICYT)
indicators.insead_methodology: UNESCO: total researchers (FTE) per million population. Government/institution reporting of R&D personnel
indicators.brunei_comment: Brunei 4.82 = estimated 20-25 researchers for 460K pop. VERY LOW (rank 73)
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Expand researcher positions, research grants, and international collaboration opportunities, including industry-linked research posts and doctoral pipelines.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Create a national register of R&D personnel using full-time equivalent concepts and report it consistently to UNESCO and WIPO with transparent methodology.
indicators.verification_by_mpb:
for Brunei Darussalam:

Current Metric: As of 2022, Brunei Darussalam recorded 506.18 researchers per million inhabitants.

Regional Comparison:
Singapore maintains a leading position in the region with 8,781.74 researchers per million inhabitants in 2022.
Malaysia recorded 1,218.33 researchers per million inhabitants in 2022, showing a consistent upward trend from previous years (e.g., 2,110.44 in 2018 and 1,218.33 in 2022).
indicators.submission_notes: Composite index from Yale. Brunei's carbon footprint, deforestation protection, and air quality data feed this.
indicators.brunei_challenge: High per-capita carbon emissions from O&G; limited renewable energy adoption

indicators.name: Senior Officials and Managers
indicators.definition: Legislators, senior officials, and managers (%) measures the percentage of total employment accounted for by legislators, senior officials and managers, based on ISCO Revision 2008 (or Rev. 1988 where appropriate).
indicators.csv_ministry_full: Prime Minister's Office / Ministry of Finance and Economy
indicators.source_name:
International Labour Organization ILOSTAT
World Bank, Global Jobs Indicators Database (JOIN)
indicators.submission_agency: Civil Service Commission / Department of Economic Planning & Statistics
indicators.insead_methodology: ILO: % workforce in ISCO-08 Major Group 1 (Legislators, managers and senior officials). Based on labour surveys
indicators.brunei_comment: Brunei +7.01 improvement (38.10→45.11) = RANK 14 GLOBALLY = world-class management talent
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Consolidate strong managerial talent through leadership development, succession planning, and cross-sector exposure for high-potential officials and managers.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Maintain accurate occupational coding and showcase Brunei's strength in managerial depth through well-documented labour statistics.
indicators.submission_notes: Survey-based. Social support systems, economic security, and cultural vibrancy affect this score.
indicators.brunei_challenge: Limited social/cultural diversity; limited employment satisfaction in private sector

indicators.name: Digital Skills
indicators.definition: Individuals with advanced ICT skills (%) refers to the share of youth and adults who have written a computer program using a specialised programming language in the last three months, expressed as a percentage of individuals that have undertaken computer-related activities during that period.
indicators.insead_methodology: ITU Digital Skills Survey (proprietary). % population with basic/advanced digital skills. Data NOT publicly available; score appears to be default ceiling
indicators.brunei_comment: CRITICAL: Perfect score 100 contradicts other indicators (software 3.02, apps 43.27). ARTIFACT
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Measure digital skills more rigorously and then scale targeted training in coding, data, cybersecurity, and applied digital tools for youth and adults.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Clarify indicator methodology with the data provider, validate country values against national ICT skills surveys, and submit stronger national evidence if the current estimate appears inconsistent.
indicators.submission_notes: Composite index. Brunei's social mobility is shaped by education access, income distribution, and public service accessibility.
indicators.brunei_challenge: Wawasan-era wealth concentration; limited meritocracy in public sector promotions

indicators.name: AI Talent Concentration
indicators.definition: AI talent concentration is calculated using counts of LinkedIn members considered AI talent (those with AI skills and/or AI jobs) relative to total LinkedIn members in a country. AI talent includes members with AI job titles and those listing AI skills on their profiles.
indicators.source_name:
LinkedIn Economic Graph
OECD AI Policy Observatory
indicators.insead_methodology: LinkedIn: Relative concentration of AI talent compared to the global average across the platform.
indicators.brunei_comment: n/a - Not reported in 2025 GTCI for Brunei
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Build a small but credible AI talent base through specialised education, applied projects, industry partnerships, and incentives that retain AI-skilled professionals.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Engage international AI talent data providers, encourage accurate digital professional profiles, and publish national AI talent mapping that can be used for validation.
indicators.submission_notes: NA
indicators.brunei_challenge: NA

indicators.name: ICT Services Exports
indicators.definition: Telecommunications, computer and information services exports as a percentage of total trade, based on EBOPS 2010 code SI and total trade defined as the average of total imports and total exports of goods and commercial services.
indicators.source_name:
Global Innovation Index Database, WIPO 2025
WTO nd UN Conference on Trade & Development, Trade in Commercial Services Database
indicators.insead_methodology: WTO Trade in Services Database: ICT services exports as % of total trade services (EBOPS 2010 classification). Country reporting
indicators.brunei_comment: Brunei 2.44 = minimal ICT services sector (rank 112). Economic diversification gap
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Promote export-ready ICT services firms through incubation, standards support, market access assistance, and targeted export promotion to regional clients.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Improve service trade classification for ICT categories and reconcile national trade reporting with WTO and UN standards so ICT exports are fully captured.
indicators.submission_notes: Hard data. Brunei's trade openness is driven by O&G exports. Diversification would stabilise and potentially improve this.
indicators.brunei_challenge: Over-reliance on O&G for trade; limited non-oil exports

indicators.name: Mobile App Development
indicators.definition: Global downloads of mobile apps (per billion PPP$ GDP) refer to global downloads of mobile apps by origin of the headquarters of the developer/firm, scaled by PPP$ GDP. Downloads are compiled from major app stores and forecast models.
indicators.source_name:
Global Innovation Index Database, WIPO 2025
Sensor Tower Company (data.ia)
International Monetary Fund, World Economic Outlook Database
indicators.insead_methodology: Sensor Tower proprietary data: app downloads per billion PPP GDP. New methodology 2025, used by WIPO GII
indicators.brunei_comment: Brunei 43.27 = rank 112 = low app economy activity. Developer base ~50-100 estimated
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Support mobile app developers through hackathons, incubators, government procurement opportunities, and small grants for commercially viable digital products.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Cross-check country attribution used by app market data providers with national business registries and developer community lists so Brunei-based developers are properly counted.
indicators.submission_notes: IMD survey. Requires cross-ministry talent retention strategy.
indicators.brunei_challenge: Brain drain to Singapore and UK; limited high-skill private sector jobs

indicators.name: Intellectual Property Receipts
indicators.definition: Charges for the use of intellectual property, i.e. receipts, as a percentage of total trade (three-year average), based on EBOPS 2010 code SH (charges for the use of intellectual property not included elsewhere). For the definition of total trade, see indicator 6.2.1.
indicators.source_name: IMF Balance of Payments Statistics / WTO Trade Database
indicators.insead_methodology: IMF Balance of Payments: IP licensing revenue (royalties + licensing fees) received from abroad. % of total trade, 3 years average
indicators.brunei_comment: Brunei 0.00 = ZERO IP licensing revenue. No commercial IP market developed
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Strengthen IP commercialisation by building technology transfer capacity, raising awareness of licensing opportunities, and supporting export of protected knowledge assets.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Ensure royalties and licence fees are correctly classified in balance-of-payments reporting and coordinate between the central bank and IP office to improve measurement.
indicators.submission_notes: Composite index. ACB's public reporting and enforcement visibility directly impacts perception scores. Increase WEF EOS respondents who give high ACB visibility.
indicators.brunei_challenge: Perception of cronyism; limited independent audit and accountability mechanisms

indicators.name: High-Value Exports
indicators.definition: High-technology exports (% of manufactured exports) refers to exports of manufactures with high R&D intensity (such as computers, pharmaceuticals, scientific instruments, and electrical machinery) as a percentage of total manufactured exports.
indicators.source_name: UN Comtrade / World Bank WDI / OECD classification
indicators.insead_methodology: UN Comtrade: high-value exports (OECD R&D-intensive categories: computers, pharma, scientific instruments) as % of total. Classification-based
indicators.brunei_comment: Brunei 1.53 = rank 115 = minimal high-tech manufacturing. Commodity-dependent (oil/gas 90%)
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Increase high-value exports by supporting niche advanced manufacturing, halal premium products, and technology-enabled tradables linked to regional value chains.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Verify customs classification and international trade mappings for high-tech and high-value products, and reconcile official export data with UN Comtrade-type reporting.
indicators.submission_notes: Survey. Streamlining immigration for skilled workers, and introducing talent/investor visas would directly improve this.
indicators.brunei_challenge: Restrictive PR pathways; limited skilled worker visa flexibility

indicators.name: Software Development
indicators.definition: GitHub commits pushes received and sent (per million population, 15–69 years old) refers to the sum of batched changes received and sent by publicly-available projects on GitHub within an economy, excluding automated non-productive commits, scaled by population aged 15–69.
indicators.source_name:
GitHub via WIPO GII 2025 / old methodology
UN, Department of Economic and Social Affairs, Population Division, World Population Prospects 2024
indicators.insead_methodology: WIPO GII / GitHub: methodology CHANGED in 2025 = NOT comparable to 2023. 2023 different metric. New 2025 = GitHub commits per population
indicators.brunei_comment: Cannot assess trend due to methodology change. New score 3.02 reflects GitHub data
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Grow the domestic software development ecosystem through coding communities, bootcamps, public-sector digital projects, and stronger demand for local software services.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Encourage developers and firms to use consistent Brunei affiliations on collaborative coding platforms and engage with data compilers to validate country attribution methods.

indicators.name: New Business Density
indicators.definition: New business density is defined as the number of newly registered firms with limited liability per 1,000 working-age people (15–64 years old) per calendar year.
indicators.insead_methodology: World Bank Entrepreneurship Database: new business registrations per 1,000 working-age population. Country submission
indicators.brunei_comment: URGENT: Score 0.00 = missing data (rank 125). Brunei business census 2023 shows 6,570 enterprises
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Raise formal new business creation by simplifying registration, reducing compliance costs, and strengthening incubation, advisory, and microfinance support for entrepreneurs.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Provide business registry data to international entrepreneurship databases using their required definitions, especially on limited liability firms and working-age population denominators.

indicators.name: Scientific Journal Articles
indicators.definition: Scientific and technical journal articles refers to the number of scientific and engineering articles published in physics, biology, chemistry, mathematics, clinical medicine, biomedical research, engineering and technology, and earth and space sciences, reported per 10,000 inhabitants.
indicators.source_name:
UNESCO Science Report / Web of Science / Scopus
World Bank World Development Indicators based on National Science Foundation, Science and Engineering Indicators
indicators.insead_methodology: UNESCO/Web of Science/Scopus: research articles published in indexed journals per 10,000 population. Citation database counting
indicators.brunei_comment: Brunei +8.62 improvement (24.29→32.91) = research output growing (rank 37). UBD investment success
indicators.how_to_improve_score: Increase scientific publication output through performance-based research funding, collaboration incentives, and targeted support for publishing in indexed journals.
indicators.how_to_improve_reporting: Ensure all Brunei-affiliated publications use consistent institutional naming, monitor indexing coverage in Scopus/Web of Science, and share updated publication metadata with relevant international bodies.
